title: Create onboarding flow
description: Create the frontend onboarding flow (Category: Frontend) to guide new users through key initial steps. Implement a welcome screen, account sign-up/sign-in shortcuts, and a brief guided walkthrough (tooltips or modal) that highlights the top 3 features with an option to skip. Integrate with existing authentication endpoints and emit an onboarding completion event to analytics. Ensure responsive layout, basic accessibility (keyboard navigable, readable labels), and include lightweight unit/visual checks. Deliverables: implemented components/screens, brief dev notes on integration points, and test verification. Estimated effort: 2.0 hours. Priority: medium. Status: pending. Assigned to: Product Team.

title: Improve onboarding flow
description: Improve onboarding flow by updating frontend screens to reduce user friction: simplify welcome copy, make primary CTA more prominent, ensure form fields have clear inline validation and error messages, and fix responsive/layout issues on mobile. Verify onboarding completion analytics event fires and include brief unit/visual checks. Deliver updated React components with a descriptive PR ready for QA. Status: pending; Assigned to Product Team; Priority: medium; Estimated effort: 2.0 hours; Category: Frontend.

title: Integrate payment provider
description: Connect checkout flow with external payment API.
risk_analysis: Main risks: technically, integrating an external payment API introduces security and correctness risks (handling tokens/PCI-sensitive data, TLS/certificate issues, idempotency, webhook delivery, error handling, retries/backoff, and differences between sandbox and production behavior) and dependency on the quality/compatibility of the provider’s SDKs and API versioning; schedule risks include an optimistic 8-hour estimate that may not cover time required for obtaining API credentials, security reviews, end-to-end testing, QA signoff and debugging intermittent failures, any of which can easily extend the timeline; dependency risks stem from external provider availability, rate limits, unexpected breaking changes, and the need to coordinate with other teams (frontend for checkout UI, DevOps for deployment/secrets and QA/legal for compliance/contracts). To mitigate, secure test credentials and provider SLAs up front, build integration behind a feature flag, implement robust retry/idempotency and logging/monitoring, allocate buffer time for security/review and end-to-end tests, and confirm cross-team responsibilities and deployment windows before starting.
risk_mitigation:
Security & compliance
- Use the provider’s hosted/tokenization flow (or their PCI-compliant SDK) so raw card data never touches our servers.
- Obtain test and production API credentials, webhook signing secrets, and any IP allow-lists before development starts; store all secrets in the team vault with least-privilege access and automated rotation.
- Enforce TLS >=1.2, strict certificate validation, and up-to-date HTTP clients; log TLS/certificate failures for early detection.
- Verify and enforce webhook signatures, timestamps, and replay protection; never accept unsigned events.
- Mask PCI-sensitive fields in logs/telemetry and define a logging policy reviewed by Security/Compliance.

Correctness & reliability
- Require idempotency keys on create/payment requests and persist transaction state to support safe retries and reconciliation.
- Implement retries with exponential backoff + jitter for transient errors, and immediately stop/rethrow for client errors (4xx) per provider guidance.
- Add a circuit-breaker/fallback to prevent cascading failures when the provider is degraded.
- Implement durable webhook handling: enqueue incoming events, process idempotently, and move repeatedly failing events to a dead-letter queue for manual review.
- Build automated reconciliation jobs to detect and resolve mismatches between our records and provider records.

Observability & monitoring
- Emit structured, non-PII metrics and traces: request latency, error rates, retry counts, webhook failures, and rate-limit responses.
- Create dashboards and alerts (SLOs) for key failure modes (spike in declines, webhook delivery failures, increased latency, producer rate-limit errors).
- Correlate logs/traces with a request-level correlation ID carried through checkout → provider → webhook for fast troubleshooting.

Testing & environment parity
- Keep sandbox/staging accounts that mirror production settings (rate-limits, signing behavior) and exercise them as part of CI.
- Write automated integration tests that mock provider responses and also run end-to-end tests against a staging provider account including webhooks, declines, retries, and edge cases.
- Maintain a contract/integration test suite that runs when updating provider SDKs or API versions.

Dependencies & vendor management
- Confirm provider SLAs, support contacts, escalation path, and known maintenance windows before go-live; document provider rate limits and quota rules.
- Pin provider SDK and API version; treat version upgrades as a scheduled change with test plan and rollback.
- Maintain a small fallback plan (e.g., temporary maintenance page or queue mode) for provider outages and test it.

Schedule & process controls
- Add explicit timeboxes to the estimate: obtain credentials (1–2 workdays), security review/pen test (2–4 workdays), end-to-end QA (1–2 workdays), and a contingency buffer (at least +50% of dev estimate or +2 workdays).
- Build the integration behind a feature flag and plan a staged rollout (internal canary → small % of traffic → full rollout).
- Define completion criteria: successful e2e checkout tests, webhook verification, security signoff, QA signoff, monitoring in place, and rollback/runbook validated.

Cross-team coordination & responsibilities
- Run a short kickoff with Frontend, DevOps, QA, Security/Compliance, and Legal to confirm responsibilities, deployment windows, and acceptance criteria.
- Assign owners for: front-end integration, secrets/deployment, monitoring/alerts, and partner support escalation.
- Schedule a deployment window with DevOps and notify QA and Security of the rollout and validation checklist.

Runbook & incident preparedness
- Create a concise runbook: quick rollback steps (toggle feature flag), how to disable provider calls, where to find logs/metrics, and how to reconcile payments manually.
- Maintain provider support contacts and a documented escalation path in the runbook.
- Run a short simulated incident drill before wider rollout (e.g., simulate webhook failures or provider rate-limiting) and iterate on the runbook.

Documentation & maintenance
- Document integration design, failure modes, test cases, and reconciliation procedures in the team wiki.
- Schedule periodic reviews: SDK/API version check every quarter and post-mortem reviews for any production payment incidents.

Priority checklist to start
- Get test/production credentials + SLA/support contacts.
- Create vault entries and restrict access.
- Design idempotency and state persistence approach and implement feature flag.
- Implement webhook verification, retries/backoff, and logging.
- Run e2e tests in staging, ship behind feature flag, monitor canary, then widen rollout.
